subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear, logical structure with wellâlabelled sections and figures
Full table of contents enabling easy navigation
Coherent narrative linking introduction, modelling, analysis, and conclusion
weaknesses: Occasional digressions and lengthy personal anecdotes interrupt flow
Repetition between main text and Appendix reduces conciseness
Minor formatting inconsistencies (broken tables, varied reference styles)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is well organized and coherent overall (clear headings, figures, table of contents) but falls short of âexceptionally polishedâ due to narrative bulk, repeated material in the Appendix, and formatting inconsistencies. These factors justify a bandâ3 award.

strengths: Consistent use of appropriate notation (e.g., exponents, ln, Ï for RSS)
Effective variety of representations (data tables, scatter plots, algebraic derivations)
Logical progression of mathematical arguments and clear linking between models and data
weaknesses: Occasional minor lapses in notation (typos like âderivateâ, inconsistent bold/italics)
Some axis labels are small or lack units
Key symbols (e.g., s in T^L(p)) are not always redefined when introduced
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language and notation are used mostly consistently and accurately, and multiple representations effectively support the analysis. Minor lapses do not impede understanding, placing this work at the top of bandâ4 for Mathematical Communication.

strengths: Exploration is clearly motivated by the studentâs own cycling experience
Independent data collection over 30 trials shows initiative
Use of RSS to compare models demonstrates sound personal engagement
weaknesses: Approach follows a conventional IA template rather than an innovative structure
Modelling choices (exponential vs. logarithmic) are standard classroom techniques
Limited introduction of novel methods beyond expected syllabus work
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs personal cycling context and selfâcollected data demonstrate clear engagement and independent thinking, but the techniques and structure remain within normal expectations, corresponding to bandâ2.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on data scatter, measurement limitations, and model fit throughout
Thoughtful analysis using RSS to justify model choice
Clear evaluation of results with realistic extension suggestions
weaknesses: Reflection lacks quantitative treatment of uncertainty or error margins
Limited depth in linking physical principles (drag, gearing) to mathematical results
Future implications are noted but not developed into concrete mathematical plans
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is distributed and thoughtful, offering meaningful insights into limitations and extensions, but it stops short of deep, sustained critical analysis and comprehensive evaluation, meriting a bandâ2 score.

strengths: Advanced use of exponential and logarithmic modelling beyond syllabus requirements
Application of RSS minimization and differentiation to real data with 30 trials
Expert handling of SLâlevel mathematics (regression, lnâmanipulation, derivative optimization)
weaknesses: Minor arithmetic and labelling inconsistencies in residual tables
Assumptions (e.g., zero asymptote) could be more explicitly justified
Some coefficient calculations exhibit small rounding slips
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration applies a wide range of SLâlevel mathematicsâregression, RSS optimization, differentiationâand extends beyond standard coursework in a rigorous manner. Despite minor errors, the mathematics is handled expertly throughout, warranting the top bandâ6 award.

strandSelections.reasoning: Explanations generally follow a logical order and justification is provided for each methodological step.  Some algebraic working is cramped and a few transitions (e.g., why ln model must be negative) are not fully explicit, yet the overall communication is clear and easy to follow, meeting the âgood logical structureâ descriptor.

The student demonstrates good conceptual understanding: selects suitable functions, manipulates logs correctly, and differentiates accurately.  Minor inaccuracies (labelling the first derivative as âslope increaseâ, slight algebra slips in tables) are present but do not undermine the overall mathematical validity, fitting the descriptor âgood understanding with minor errorsâ.

The student employs tables of raw data, scatter plots, algebraic equations, derivative tables, and written explanation.  Each representation is clearly linked to the others (e.g., scatter plot â regression line â residual table).  The variety is effective, though axis labels are occasionally small and no units appear on some derivative tables, keeping it at âgoodâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ.

The report maintains a clear narrative: introduction â aim â data â modelling â analysis â conclusion.  Explanations generally follow from one another and the reader can trace how each decision (choice of function, use of RSS, selection of optimum) connects to the stated aim.  Nonetheless, occasional digressions (lengthy personal anecdotes, repeated statements that models are âtoo linearâ) interrupt the logical thread, so coherence is solid but not exceptional.

Using RSS to compare models and devising a pedal-coasting strategy show some creativity and initiative.  However, the mathematical techniques remain within expected classroom tools and no particularly novel method is introduced, so the work fits âgoodâ rather than âexceptionalâ.

Results are compared via RSS, and the choice of logarithmic model is justified quantitatively.  Nevertheless, the analysis of why the model is appropriate (physics of drag, gearing, human power curves) is only briefly mentioned, so the level of critical depth is thoughtful but not âdeep and insightfulâ.

The student evaluates the outcome (47 pedals is optimal, 17â25 % time saving) and proposes realistic extensions (gear ratios, better measurement).  Future implications are noted, but quantification of error or confidence intervals is absent, putting the evaluation solidly in the âclearâ rather than âcomprehensiveâ band.

The student reflects on data scatter, limitations (road, friction, physique) and acknowledges uncertainty, showing meaningful reflection.  Reflection is distributed throughout rather than being a superficial add-on, but does not reach the depth of sustained critical questioning expected for the top band.

Functions are written with appropriate notation (Tá´±(p)=43.5e^{-0.0167p}, Tâ²(p)=â11.75/p), logs are expressed with âlnâ, and Ï notation is used for RSS.  A few slips such as âderivateâ and inconsistent bold/italics show minor lapses, but overall the mathematical language is mostly correct and consistent.

The exploration is clearly rooted in the studentâs personal cycling experience and tailored to a route the student actually rides.  This establishes a personal approach, yet the write-up still follows a conventional IA template rather than a highly innovative format.

While the majority of content is relevant, long narrative paragraphs on childhood interest, repeated justification of model choice, and step-by-step algebra that could be summarised add noticeable bulk.  At 13 pages the essay could be tighter; therefore it is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ rather than genuinely concise.

Logarithmic and exponential modelling, use of e, derivatives, RSS, and percentage gradients are all relevant to the aim and are applied effectively to draw a conclusion.  The mathematics goes beyond the basic syllabus (RSS is not explicitly taught) but is not wide-ranging enough to be considered âcomprehensiveâ or âexpertâ.

Designing and conducting 30 self-recorded trials, selecting two different functional forms, and constructing an RSS test demonstrate noticeable independent thinking, but the modelling choices (simple exponential / log regressions) remain standard class-level techniques.

A full table of contents, labelled figures, and section headings (Introduction, Data Collection, Modelling, RSS, Conclusion, Extension) give the exploration a recognisable structure that is easy to navigate.  The reader can locate information quickly and each section fulfils a clear purpose.  Minor weaknessesâe.g., tables broken by pageâwrap and an Appendix that repeats explanations already in the textâprevent the piece from feeling âexceptionallyâ polished.

All techniques (regression, differentiation of ln p, use of Ï notation) are firmly within the SL course and are used correctly.  The complexity is appropriate and no procedure is trivial or below level.

annotations.comment: The transformation in function Tá´±(p)=be^{cp} is introduced without deriving from first principles; explicitly solve for b and c using simultaneous equations.
When stating the aim, explicitly define dependent and independent variables. For example, âLet p be the number of pedals, and T(p) be time in secondsâ to avoid ambiguity.
The introductory paragraph contains lengthy personal anecdotes that obscure the mathematical focus. Consider condensing narrative to highlight rationale and aim more succinctly.
The second paragraph elaborates on personal experience but adds limited academic value. Trim or integrate details that directly support the explorationâs mathematical rationale.
The dataset table omits units for time and lacks uncertainty estimates. Include âseconds (s)â in the header and comment on stopwatch precision.
The assumption that the horizontal asymptote is y=0 ignores human reaction time; consider adding a constant vertical shift âaâ instead of dismissing it.
References follow varied formats and lack consistent presentation. Apply a single citation style (e.g., APA) and include full publication details.
The hypothesis description uses qualitative language (âmajor time gainsâ); refine into a testable mathematical statement, such as âTâ²(p) approaches zero at optimal p.â
The description of the âminimum pedalsâ (24) lacks justification. Explain how this threshold was determined and discuss potential measurement error.
Fig 1 caption lacks a scale or dimensions on the image of the test track. Add axis scales or reference markers to clarify measurement.
The scatter plotâs axis labels are very small and lack units. Ensure both axes clearly state âNumber of Pedalsâ and âTime (s)â with legible font.
The narrative notes âsubstantial uncertaintyâ but does not quantify it. Reflect on how measurement error could affect model parameters and optimal p.
Rounding the exponential model to three significant figures introduces error; record full values through calculations and round only final results.
Applying only two data points to estimate the exponential model is less robust than least-squares regression. Consider fitting via software to minimize RSS directly.
Fig 3 effectively compares model to data but lacks residual plots. Include residuals to assess systematic deviation.
In defining the logarithmic model, b is dismissed without showing algebraic reasoning. Clarify how asymptote location fixes b and c.
The prepared function T^L(p) = (aâln(p)+c)/s introduces s without prior definition. Ensure all symbols have clear meaning.
The table of logarithmic derivation is cluttered; separate the algebraic steps from final function for clarity and label equations (1), (2), etc.
Coefficient simplification yields T^L(p)=73.8â14.1âln(p), but earlier a was 11.16/ln(24/53)ââ? Check arithmetic consistency to avoid incorrect factors.
Switching to T^{CE} and T^{CLE} without reâstating their definitions interrupts reader comprehension. Reâintroduce names when changing notation.
RSS formula defines R_i and X_i vaguely. Explicitly state R_i = observed time, X_i = predicted time, and include summation indices.
In Table 2, some squared residuals appear negative or zero inconsistently. Verify that all r^2 entries are nonânegative and correctly computed.
Derivative table headers (T' and T^{ICL}) are not clearly defined; include a legend explaining which model each column refers to.
The selection of 47 pedals as âoptimalâ is based on qualitative slope change; strengthen with a quantitative stopping rule, e.g., |Tâ²(p)|<0.01.
Conclusion focuses on personal benefits but should tie back to limitations and suggest confidence in results (e.g., error margins).
Extension section lists many factors but lacks a plan or mathematical approach to incorporate them. Propose methods to model road gradient or drag quantitatively.
The Appendix repeats definitions of friction and drag that are already described. Integrate brief definitions into main text or remove to maintain conciseness.
